title: Menopause & HRT
oneLiner: Perimenopause/menopause is natural oestrogen withdrawal causing vasomotor, urogenital and mood symptoms; HRT is first-line for symptom relief and bone health.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **VTE (PE/DVT):** Unilateral leg swelling + dyspnoea + pleuritic chest pain → HRT contraindicated until VTE treated & anticoagulation stable
- **Endometrial cancer:** Postmenopausal bleeding (PMB) — any bleeding >12 months after LMP → must refer for TVUS & biopsy before starting oestrogen
- **Ovarian cancer:** Abdominal bloating + early satiety + pelvic mass → HRT may accelerate growth; wait for histology
- **MI/stroke in perimenopausal woman:** Atypical presentation (nausea, fatigue, epigastric pain) — do NOT assume HRT vasomotor symptoms; check ECG & troponin
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal symptoms: Vasomotor (hot flushes, night sweats), sleep disturbance, vaginal dryness/dyspareunia, mood swings/low mood, joint pain, reduced libido
- Key exam findings: Usually normal; vaginal atrophy (pale, friable mucosa), atrophic vaginitis; may have elevated BP if undiagnosed HTN
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- Most likely -> less likely:
  - Perimenopause (irregular cycles + symptoms) — FSH raised, but not required if clinical
  - Primary ovarian insufficiency (POI) — age <40 years, amenorrhoea + FSH >25 IU/L
  - Pregnancy — amenorrhoea + nausea — always test
  - Thyroid dysfunction — weight change, tremor, goitre — TSH/fT4
  - Depression/ anxiety — mood predominant, lack of vasomotor symptoms
  - Other causes of amenorrhoea (e.g. hypothalamic, prolactinoma) — hx of eating disorder, galactorrhoea, PRL level
- **Splitter:** Hot flushes + irregular cycles → perimenopause; no vasomotor symptoms + isolated amenorrhoea → consider structural/ endocrine cause
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Urine β-hCG to exclude pregnancy before HRT; BP check (HTN may worsen with HRT)
- **Bloods:** FSH (elevated >25 IU/L if clinical uncertainty), LH, oestradiol (low); TSH if thyroid symptoms; prolactin if galactorrhoea or amenorrhoea; check lipids & LFT if starting oral oestrogen (first-pass hepatic effect)
- **Imaging:** TVUS if PMB (<=12 months from LMP) — endo thickness >4mm often need biopsy; pelvic US if suspicious symptoms/mass
- **Special tests:** Bone density (DEXA) if early menopause (<45 years) or fracture risk
- **Diagnostic criteria:** STRAW+10 staging for ovarian ageing; clinical diagnosis if age 45-55 + ≥2 typical symptoms (no test needed)
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** — cannot start HRT if pregnant; **TSH** — untreated hyperthyroidism can mimic menopause symptoms
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Exclude mimics (pregnancy, thyroid, VTE, malignancy)
  - Do NOT start HRT if undiagnosed PMB, active VTE, or h/o hormone-sensitive cancer (breast, endometrial)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - If symptomatic & no contraindications: initiate HRT — oestrogen + progestogen if uterus intact (endometrial protection)
  - For urogenital symptoms only: vaginal oestrogen (e.g. estriol cream, pessaries) — safe even with contraindications to systemic HRT
  - Offer non-hormonal options: SSRIs (paroxetine), gabapentin, clonidine for vasomotor symptoms
  - Patient education: discuss risks (VTE, breast cancer, stroke) vs benefits (symptom relief, bone health, QoL)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Review at 3 months; then annually — BP, breast exam, mammogram (if age>50), bone density if risk factors
  - HRT typically continued for 2-5 years; longer if persistent symptoms or low BMD
  - Taper gradually if discontinuing (cold turkey may cause rebound symptoms)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Usually outpatient, but if admitted for other reason: continue HRT (do not stop abruptly); monitor BP; VTE prophylaxis if immobilised
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents — systemic:**
  - **Oestrogen (patches):** Estradiol patch (e.g. Evorel 25-100 mcg/24h) — apply twice weekly; no first-pass effect → lower VTE risk
  - **Oestrogen (oral):** Estradiol valerate 1-2 mg daily or conjugated equine oestrogens 0.3-1.25 mg daily — higher VTE risk than transdermal
  - **Progestogen (if uterus intact):** Micronised progesterone (Utrogestan) 200 mg daily for 12-14 days/cycle; or levonorgestrel IUS (Mirena) for endometrial protection
- **First-line — urogenital:**
  - Estriol cream (Ovestin) 1 applicatorful daily for 2 weeks then twice weekly; or estradiol vaginal ring (Estring)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Tibolone (Livial) 2.5 mg daily — synthetic steroid; useful if cannot tolerate progestogen; check LFTs
  - Clonidine 50-75 mcg bd for vasomotor symptoms (non-hormonal)
  - SSRIs: paroxetine 10-20 mg daily; venlafaxine ER 75-150 mg daily
- **Key contraindications:**
  - Active VTE, history of hormone-sensitive cancer (breast, endometrial), undiagnosed PMB, severe active liver disease
  - Migraine with aura (prefer transdermal, lower stroke risk)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose change for renal impairment; in liver disease, avoid oral oestrogen (first-pass) → use transdermal
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Starting oral oestrogen without progestogen in a woman with a uterus** → endometrial hyperplasia → cancer. Always confirm hysterectomy status before prescribing unopposed oestrogen.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - VTE (risk higher with oral oestrogen, especially first year)
  - Breast tenderness, bloating, nausea (usually transient)
  - Irregular bleeding (common in first 3-6 months; investigate if persists)
- **Chronic:**
  - Breast cancer (risk increase 1.5-2x with combined HRT >5 years; falls after stopping)
  - Endometrial cancer (unopposed oestrogen)
  - Ovarian cancer (small increased risk with long-term use)
  - Stroke risk (oral oestrogen; transdermal lower risk)
  - Gallbladder disease (oral oestrogen)
sections.Prognosis:
- Vasomotor symptoms typically last 4-7 years; HRT reduces them by 75-90% within 4 weeks
- Bone loss accelerated in first 5 years post-menopause — HRT maintains BMD; fracture risk reduced with long-term use
- Most women discontinue HRT after 2-5 years; symptoms may return; re-start if needed
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always confirm hysterectomy status** before prescribing HRT — uterus intact = add progestogen (or Mirena IUS)
- **Choose transdermal route** in women with VTE risk, migraine with aura, or liver disease — lower stroke/VTE risk
- **Offer vaginal oestrogen** even if contraindication to systemic HRT — minimal systemic absorption, safe in breast cancer history (discuss with oncologist)
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Starting HRT without ruling out pregnancy or PMB** — can mask underlying malignancy
- **Using oral oestrogen in a woman with previous VTE** — use transdermal with anticoagulation or avoid entirely
- **Tapering HRT abruptly** — can cause severe rebound vasomotor symptoms; advise gradual reduction over 3-6 months
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - New vaginal bleeding after 6 months of HRT or spotting >12 months after LMP
  - Unexplained leg swelling/pain or sudden dyspnoea (possible VTE)
  - Severe headache, visual changes, or focal neurological signs (possible stroke/TIA)
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 3 months to assess symptom control & BP; then annual review; mammogram if age 50+; DEXA if early menopause or fracture risk
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / gynae on-call if:** PMB + abnormal TVUS (endo >4mm) or suspected endometrial cancer; any acute VTE while on HRT; severe headache/visual symptoms (possible stroke/TIA)
- **Call crash team / ICU if:** NEWS2 >5; massive PE (hypotension, shock, high lactate); suspected stroke with GCS <15

rawMarkdown:
# Menopause & HRT
**One-liner:** Perimenopause/menopause is natural oestrogen withdrawal causing vasomotor, urogenital and mood symptoms; HRT is first-line for symptom relief and bone health.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **VTE (PE/DVT):** Unilateral leg swelling + dyspnoea + pleuritic chest pain → HRT contraindicated until VTE treated & anticoagulation stable
- **Endometrial cancer:** Postmenopausal bleeding (PMB) — any bleeding >12 months after LMP → must refer for TVUS & biopsy before starting oestrogen
- **Ovarian cancer:** Abdominal bloating + early satiety + pelvic mass → HRT may accelerate growth; wait for histology
- **MI/stroke in perimenopausal woman:** Atypical presentation (nausea, fatigue, epigastric pain) — do NOT assume HRT vasomotor symptoms; check ECG & troponin

### Presentation
- Cardinal symptoms: Vasomotor (hot flushes, night sweats), sleep disturbance, vaginal dryness/dyspareunia, mood swings/low mood, joint pain, reduced libido
- Key exam findings: Usually normal; vaginal atrophy (pale, friable mucosa), atrophic vaginitis; may have elevated BP if undiagnosed HTN

### Differential Diagnosis
- Most likely -> less likely:
  - Perimenopause (irregular cycles + symptoms) — FSH raised, but not required if clinical
  - Primary ovarian insufficiency (POI) — age <40 years, amenorrhoea + FSH >25 IU/L
  - Pregnancy — amenorrhoea + nausea — always test
  - Thyroid dysfunction — weight change, tremor, goitre — TSH/fT4
  - Depression/ anxiety — mood predominant, lack of vasomotor symptoms
  - Other causes of amenorrhoea (e.g. hypothalamic, prolactinoma) — hx of eating disorder, galactorrhoea, PRL level
- **Splitter:** Hot flushes + irregular cycles → perimenopause; no vasomotor symptoms + isolated amenorrhoea → consider structural/ endocrine cause

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Urine β-hCG to exclude pregnancy before HRT; BP check (HTN may worsen with HRT)
- **Bloods:** FSH (elevated >25 IU/L if clinical uncertainty), LH, oestradiol (low); TSH if thyroid symptoms; prolactin if galactorrhoea or amenorrhoea; check lipids & LFT if starting oral oestrogen (first-pass hepatic effect)
- **Imaging:** TVUS if PMB (<=12 months from LMP) — endo thickness >4mm often need biopsy; pelvic US if suspicious symptoms/mass
- **Special tests:** Bone density (DEXA) if early menopause (<45 years) or fracture risk
- **Diagnostic criteria:** STRAW+10 staging for ovarian ageing; clinical diagnosis if age 45-55 + ≥2 typical symptoms (no test needed)
- **Never-miss test:** **Pregnancy test** — cannot start HRT if pregnant; **TSH** — untreated hyperthyroidism can mimic menopause symptoms

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Exclude mimics (pregnancy, thyroid, VTE, malignancy)
  - Do NOT start HRT if undiagnosed PMB, active VTE, or h/o hormone-sensitive cancer (breast, endometrial)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - If symptomatic & no contraindications: initiate HRT — oestrogen + progestogen if uterus intact (endometrial protection)
  - For urogenital symptoms only: vaginal oestrogen (e.g. estriol cream, pessaries) — safe even with contraindications to systemic HRT
  - Offer non-hormonal options: SSRIs (paroxetine), gabapentin, clonidine for vasomotor symptoms
  - Patient education: discuss risks (VTE, breast cancer, stroke) vs benefits (symptom relief, bone health, QoL)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Review at 3 months; then annually — BP, breast exam, mammogram (if age>50), bone density if risk factors
  - HRT typically continued for 2-5 years; longer if persistent symptoms or low BMD
  - Taper gradually if discontinuing (cold turkey may cause rebound symptoms)
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Usually outpatient, but if admitted for other reason: continue HRT (do not stop abruptly); monitor BP; VTE prophylaxis if immobilised

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents — systemic:**
  - **Oestrogen (patches):** Estradiol patch (e.g. Evorel 25-100 mcg/24h) — apply twice weekly; no first-pass effect → lower VTE risk
  - **Oestrogen (oral):** Estradiol valerate 1-2 mg daily or conjugated equine oestrogens 0.3-1.25 mg daily — higher VTE risk than transdermal
  - **Progestogen (if uterus intact):** Micronised progesterone (Utrogestan) 200 mg daily for 12-14 days/cycle; or levonorgestrel IUS (Mirena) for endometrial protection
- **First-line — urogenital:**
  - Estriol cream (Ovestin) 1 applicatorful daily for 2 weeks then twice weekly; or estradiol vaginal ring (Estring)
- **Common alternatives:**
  - Tibolone (Livial) 2.5 mg daily — synthetic steroid; useful if cannot tolerate progestogen; check LFTs
  - Clonidine 50-75 mcg bd for vasomotor symptoms (non-hormonal)
  - SSRIs: paroxetine 10-20 mg daily; venlafaxine ER 75-150 mg daily
- **Key contraindications:**
  - Active VTE, history of hormone-sensitive cancer (breast, endometrial), undiagnosed PMB, severe active liver disease
  - Migraine with aura (prefer transdermal, lower stroke risk)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose change for renal impairment; in liver disease, avoid oral oestrogen (first-pass) → use transdermal
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Starting oral oestrogen without progestogen in a woman with a uterus** → endometrial hyperplasia → cancer. Always confirm hysterectomy status before prescribing unopposed oestrogen.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - VTE (risk higher with oral oestrogen, especially first year)
  - Breast tenderness, bloating, nausea (usually transient)
  - Irregular bleeding (common in first 3-6 months; investigate if persists)
- **Chronic:**
  - Breast cancer (risk increase 1.5-2x with combined HRT >5 years; falls after stopping)
  - Endometrial cancer (unopposed oestrogen)
  - Ovarian cancer (small increased risk with long-term use)
  - Stroke risk (oral oestrogen; transdermal lower risk)
  - Gallbladder disease (oral oestrogen)

### Prognosis
- Vasomotor symptoms typically last 4-7 years; HRT reduces them by 75-90% within 4 weeks
- Bone loss accelerated in first 5 years post-menopause — HRT maintains BMD; fracture risk reduced with long-term use
- Most women discontinue HRT after 2-5 years; symptoms may return; re-start if needed

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always confirm hysterectomy status** before prescribing HRT — uterus intact = add progestogen (or Mirena IUS)
- **Choose transdermal route** in women with VTE risk, migraine with aura, or liver disease — lower stroke/VTE risk
- **Offer vaginal oestrogen** even if contraindication to systemic HRT — minimal systemic absorption, safe in breast cancer history (discuss with oncologist)

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Starting HRT without ruling out pregnancy or PMB** — can mask underlying malignancy
- **Using oral oestrogen in a woman with previous VTE** — use transdermal with anticoagulation or avoid entirely
- **Tapering HRT abruptly** — can cause severe rebound vasomotor symptoms; advise gradual reduction over 3-6 months

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - New vaginal bleeding after 6 months of HRT or spotting >12 months after LMP
  - Unexplained leg swelling/pain or sudden dyspnoea (possible VTE)
  - Severe headache, visual changes, or focal neurological signs (possible stroke/TIA)
- **Follow-up:** GP review in 3 months to assess symptom control & BP; then annual review; mammogram if age 50+; DEXA if early menopause or fracture risk

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / gynae on-call if:** PMB + abnormal TVUS (endo >4mm) or suspected endometrial cancer; any acute VTE while on HRT; severe headache/visual symptoms (possible stroke/TIA)
- **Call crash team / ICU if:** NEWS2 >5; massive PE (hypotension, shock, high lactate); suspected stroke with GCS <15
